title: Fondant modelling figures
subtitle: Sculpting simple sugar-paste animals and people
excerpt: How to build basic fondant figures from a small set of repeated shapes: spheres, cones, cylinders, and sausages. The techniques that produce recognisable animals and people for cake decoration.
sourceNotes: Sugar modelling is a confectionery tradition documented from Renaissance Italy and France, where sculpted sugar figures were made for aristocratic banquets. The domestic version using ready-made sugarpaste or fondant is a twentieth-century development described in British cake-decorating guides from the 1960s onward. The techniques (sphere, cone, cylinder as the basic vocabulary) are stable across all instructional sources.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Finished figures keep for several weeks at room temperature in a dry place. They are fragile: store individually. Do not refrigerate.

glossaryTerms.definition: Sugarpaste (ready-to-roll fondant) strengthened with a small amount of CMC (carboxymethylcellulose) powder or gum tragacanth. The addition makes the paste drier and firmer, giving it the structural strength to hold thin shapes without sagging. Standard sugarpaste works for simple shapes; modelling paste is used for fine details such as fingers, ears, and standing figures.

content.text: Fondant modelling has a small vocabulary: sphere, cone, cylinder, teardrop, and sausage. Every recognisable figure is built from combinations of these shapes. Simple animals (hedgehog, teddy, bird) and basic human figures all use the same five forms. For anything with fine detail or thin standing elements, use
rather than plain sugarpaste: it holds its shape as it dries instead of sagging under its own weight.

Edible glue (tylose paste dissolved in water, or cooled boiled water) is the adhesive for joining parts. Plain water works for small joins. Use a cocktail stick to hold standing heads and ears in place while the glue dries. Don't rush the drying: a head attached to a body before the body has firmed will cause the whole figure to tilt.

Knead modelling paste until warm and smooth. Roll a piece between your palms in a circular motion to form a sphere. Apply even downward pressure with one palm to flatten a sphere into a disc. Roll a sphere lightly on the work surface to elongate it into a sausage. Roll one end of a sausage more thinly than the other to form a teardrop. Roll a sphere into a cylinder by pressing lightly with both palms moving apart. These five shapes (sphere, disc, sausage, teardrop, cylinder) are the complete vocabulary.

Build from the largest part outward. For a standing teddy: make a large sphere for the body, flatten the base so it sits level. Make a smaller sphere for the head. Make two small spheres for ears, indented with a fingertip. Make four sausages for limbs. Let the body firm for 30 minutes before attaching the head. Join each part with a small dab of edible glue. Facial features are added last: pupils of black paste, a tiny pink nose sphere, a curved line of white paste for a smile.

Place finished figures on baking paper and leave to dry at room temperature for at least 2 hours before handling or placing on a cake. Figures can be dusted with edible lustre powder or painted with gel food colouring diluted in a small amount of clear alcohol (vodka) or water once fully dry.
